summary: Gruber runs through rumors ahead of Apple's September 2022 'Far Out' event, focusing on iPhone 14 naming conventions and Apple Watch branding questions. He argues that 'Plus' fits better than 'Max' for the non-Pro large iPhone because 'Max' implies premium technical sophistication in Apple's branding language. He's skeptical that the rugged Apple Watch will be called 'Pro,' since that name doesn't convey ruggedness and conflicts with existing luxury models. He also predicts modest price increases across the iPhone 14 lineup, suggesting Apple is deliberately widening the gap between regular and Pro models. His core thesis is that Apple's naming and pricing decisions reflect a deliberate strategy to differentiate product tiers by capability, not just size.
key_points: The iPhone Mini is being replaced by a larger non-Pro model, likely called iPhone 14 Plus rather than Max, because 'Max' in Apple parlance implies premium sophistication beyond just size
Gruber is skeptical the rugged Apple Watch will be called 'Pro' since that name connotes superiority and luxury, which conflicts with a chunky, sports-oriented device
Apple is predicted to raise iPhone prices modestly while widening the feature and price gap between regular and Pro models
Apple prioritizes profit margin over revenue, meaning the cheaper iPhone 14 Plus could be as profitable as the Pro Max
Apple's naming conventions carry real semantic weight that reflects product positioning strategy
money_quotes: Max, in Apple parlance, seems to mean more than just bigger — it implies a certain premium-priced technical sophistication as well.
Sometimes when Apple describes a product as 'pro', they really just mean 'nicer in some ways', not necessarily 'better suited for professional usage'.
Are AirPods Pro more professional than regular AirPods? No, but they're more pro in Apple parlance, because they're nicer.
'Pro' carries a connotation of superiority, so I can't quite see Apple using that name for a bigger, chunkier, decidedly un-luxurious model.
Oh, how about 'Apple Watch Max'? That works for me. Skips right over 'Pro' and goes right to 'big ass and more expensive'.
Apple keeps its eye on the real prize: profit margin, not revenue.
I think the iPhone 14 Plus, even if $250 cheaper than the 14 Pro Max, will be as profitable, if not more so.
key_insight: Apple's product naming is a deliberate branding language where each word — Plus, Pro, Max — carries specific connotations about capability, luxury, and sophistication that constrain how new products can be positioned.
